narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away because a model sees a 51% chance you'll struggle with the bar exam feels unfair and personal. Picture the model as an experienced admissions advisor who's reviewed thousands of profiles like yours, piecing together a story from the details you shared. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 1.0, placing you at the bottom of your class that year, which painted the strongest picture of potential challenges ahead on the bar. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 33.0, which, while solid for some paths, signaled to the model a tougher climb in the rigorous bar prep compared to higher scorers. Your Year 1 decile of 1.0 reinforced this, suggesting early academic hurdles that carried through. Layered on top was your low family income, which the model linked to added barriers many students face without extra resources for tutoring or support. Your Asian background, unfortunately, aligned in the data with patterns of heightened competition and pressure that sometimes tip toward failure rates. Even your undergraduate GPA of 3.1 and full-time study plans pulled in the same direction, as did being female in this predictive lens—each a small thread weaving into a riskier overall tapestry. It's not that any one thing doomed you, but this particular combination echoed stories of students who, despite their determination, hit roadblocks on the bar. The model isn't judging your spirit or potential—it’s just highlighting where history shows the odds stack up. You have real strengths here, like committing to full-time focus, and with targeted steps like LSAT retakes or grade-boosting strategies, you could rewrite this story for another school. Hang in there; this is one chapter, not the whole book.
